text:
Powered byOutside

(Photo Hope Tech)

When I think aboutHopeI think of mountain bike tech and incredible four-piston brakes. That’s far from the full story though.



Hope is a distinctly British brand and an expert in CNC machining. The brand makes almost every part of a bike and there are even a few carbon fiber framesets included in that. One of those frames is a wild aero track frame that first came to light in the lead up to the Tokyo Olympics. For the2024 Paris Games, there are some new details and I’ve had a chance to interview one of the Hope Tech Design engineers who helped bring it to life. Admittedly he was tight lipped, but there’s still plenty to reveal.

2019 feels like a lifetime ago, but with the Tokyo Olympics in the future, Hope started working with Lotus, Renishaw, and British cycling to make something extraordinary. The idea was to bring the best of British engineering to bear on a bike. At the time, recently changed UCI rules allowed forks and seat stays to be up to 8 cm wide and it created an opportunity.

Time was of the essence though. In order to qualify for the Olympics a bike has to be available for sale and see competition ahead of time. If Team Britain was going to ride a wild British aero creation, it needed UCI approval and athletes had to have saddle time during the 2019/2020 Tissot UCI Track Cycling World Cup series.

Things were so tight that I’m told by Sam Pendred, design engineer with Hope Tech, that British Cycling hadn’t counted on using this new super bike. According to Pendred, “British Cycling were initially working on a few project with us in the run up to the Tokyo Olympic Games and after a few factory visits they realized the capabilities we have to develop a track bike due to our in-house mold making and carbon frame manufacturing set up.

“We initially started working with them to prototype the wide fork/stay design that British Cycling had as a plan B project that would be used in Paris. Plan B soon became plan A with their successful testing in the wind tunnel and our carbon test frame we manufactured. We then got all of the molds completed for the frames and wheels alongside developing the composite laminate with the highest spec material to get all the frames and wheels completed for the Tokyo Olympic Games.”

Of course Hope Tech wasn’t the only player. Hope handles the manufacturing and Ian Weatherill, managing director with Hope Technology says, “the in‐house team has unrivaled engineering expertise with 30 years of composite experience and two Olympics behind them.”

British car brand Lotus meanwhile handles the “front forks and handlebars for the new bike, working with Hope to integrate these components into the overall package. This has included a full program of wind tunnel evaluation on both bike and rider, plus testing to minimize the weight while maximizing the strength. Lotus has also worked on improving stiffness and front end feel to boost rider confidence.” Then there’s Renishaw to handle any additive manufacturing needs.

As with any bike though, the rider is an important piece. Hope describes the contribution of British Cycling beyond just the technology piece. Athletes are an important part of the development too.

“The bike has been developed in partnership with experienced riders from the GBCT with the aim of trying to get a right‐first‐time feel and fit‐for‐purpose track bike that the team will use,” Hope says.

All this came together in a radical super wide design and results. As with theLook P24, the fork legs and seat stays are widely spaced to match a rider’s legs. Hope shared that the final product lacked any paint or filler as “Lotus and Hope are proud of the quality of construction and wanted the opportunity to show it off.” The bike not only saw use at the 2020 Olympics in Tokyo but British athletes used the Hope HBT to bring home seven medals.

Unlike the first generation, updates for this year are less radical. As with any rule book, the UCI rules only allow designs to go to a certain point. While the first generation benefited from rule changes Pendred shares that the second generation is more about refinement saying, “there will always be room for improvement, albeit with diminishing returns.” To be fair though, he also leaves room for continued improvement going on to say, “as this is still a relatively new concept in bike design there is still a lot to learn and room to develop it further.”

To help make sense of where things are right now in the development, Pendred states “due to COVID delaying the Tokyo games, there has been a shorter time frame to develop the bike so after speaking to British Cycling we worked out the best way to improve the bike and still make it possible to have something faster for the whole team in Paris. The front end was the biggest focus and we have designed our frame tooling so we can add/remove different head tube sections therefore allowing a reduced frontal area and also the integration of the stem/bars into the top tube of the frame. We also redesigned the seat stays for a drag reduction, and combined with new forks and new handlebars it has stacked up to be a significant improvement on the Tokyo frameset.”

What that actually looks like starts with fork legs. While the first generation was a ribbon like shape with hard edges, the new design uses a serrated edge. This is paired with “refinements to the headstock and seatstays,” though neither is all that noticeable.

What is noticeable is the updated seatpost. Like the Look P24, it is wide and split but it differs in that it comes back together before joining the rest of the frame. It’s reminiscent of the Trek IsoFlow design which Trek shared helps speed up the air between the rider’s legs. It’s not in quite the same location though and Hope didn’t share specifics on the reasons for any of the design details.

Along with the updated aero, Renishaw had a bit of a chance to highlight the brand’s additive manufacturing expertise as well. The cranks are a new design using 3D printed titanium and Renishaw reports “Our aim for this Olympic bike is to push the boundaries with AM technology further, whilst still achieving high-performance components that are tailored to the riders and meet Olympic requirements for strength and weight.”



Well, kind of. Ethan Vernon used it in 2021 to place seventh in the U23 World TT Championship race. At the time Vernon sounded pleased with the design that saw Hope managing to add a Shimano Dura-Ace R9200 groupset and brakes into an updated version of the first generation frame. The Renishaw crank was nowhere to be seen and Vernon ended up making a last minute, borrowed, wheel change to HED for the rear with a Campagnolo front. Other changes included a switch to Vision Trimax extensions paired with a Hope base bar and a K-edge computer mount in place of the 3D-printed pieces used in Tokyo.

Hope reports that Vittoria Bussi also used it but outside of that, it seems to have disappeared. When asked, Pendred shared that the bike “was pushed as a development for the time trial in the Tokyo Olympics but due to numerous factors it never quite made it over the line. From our testing, there is still plenty to learn and refine regarding how this design behaves out on the road, particularly down to varying conditions with a wide stance, 8cm deep fork, it requires some careful consideration to the geometry of the frameset and also the profiles used on the tubes to get the performance out on the road.

If you are a fan of radical technology, maybe don’t worry too much that the road version of the Hope HBT is gone forever. Pendred went on to say “Hopefully when resources become available this can be developed further, but a TT bike is a very different challenge to a track bike.”

The Olympics requires availability and you can buy the Hope HBT directly from Hope though it is the most expensive I’ve looked at so far. Start by choosing a frameset configured for Pursuit, Upright (Omnium), or Sprint. There are eight sizes available and each one costs £25,000 + VAT. Then if you want the Standard Lotus 3D printed fork that’s an additional £2000 + VAT. If you want to also add the Twin Seat Post that’s another £2,000 + VAT. Add all that up and you still need wheels and your options are Disc Rear (£2,700.00 inc vat), Disc Front (£2,500.00 inc vat), Trispoke Front (£2,700.00 inc vat), Hope HR Disc Rear (£4,200.00 inc vat), and Hope HR Disc Front (£4,000.00 inc vat).

If you are ready to figure out geometry check outHopetechhb.comfor more info.
title: Is the Craziest Track Bike at the Paris Olympics Coming to the Road?
author: Josh Ross
description: Co-developed with Lotus, the Hope HBT Paris frame is a moonshot aero track bike. Could it one day be the fastest TT bike around?
